title: Rosa woodsii subsp. arizonica
what_description: Rosa woodsii subsp. arizonica is a botanical subspecies of flowering plant that belongs to the family Rosaceae. It is a shrub that typically grows to 1-3 meters in height, with thornless or sparsely thorny stems and pubescent young shoots. The leaves are pinnate, with 5-7 leaflets, and are 3-6 centimeters long. The flowers are pink to red, with 5 petals, and are borne in clusters at the ends of the stems in late spring. The fruit is a red or purple pome, 1-2 centimeters in diameter, containing 3-5 seeds.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Rosa woodsii subsp. arizonica are inflammation, fever, and skin irritation, which are alleviated by its anti-inflammatory and antipyretic properties. Diabetes and diarrhea can be controlled due to its ability to regulate blood sugar levels and inhibit the growth of pathogenic bacteria in the gut. Wound healing and digestive issues are promoted by the plant's antimicrobial and antifungal properties, as well as its ability to stimulate collagen synthesis and improve gut motility. Rosa woodsii subsp. arizonica also exhibits anticancer properties, and has been shown to inhibit the growth of various cancer cell lines. Additionally, the plant has been used to treat coughs, gastroenteritis, asthma, rheumatism, fungal infections, skin infections, ulcers, infection, influenza, eczema, hypertension, and anxiety.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Rosa woodsii subsp. arizonica are its antioxidant activity, which can be attributed to the presence of flavonoids such as quercetin and kaempferol. The plant has been found to possess antimicrobial properties, effective against a range of microorganisms, including bacteria and fungi, due to the presence of saponins and volatile oils. It also exhibits anti-inflammatory activity, which may be linked to the ability of its bioactive compounds to inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes. Additionally, the plant has been reported to possess analgesic properties, thought to be the result of the interaction between its bioactive compounds and pain receptors in the body. The cardioprotective effects of Rosa woodsii subsp. arizonica may be attributed to its ability to inhibit the oxidation of low-density lipoprotein cholesterol and improve blood flow, both of which are mediated by its bioactive compounds.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Rosa woodsii subsp. arizonica are quercetin, a flavonoid antioxidant that has anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, rosmarinic acid, a diterpene that exhibits anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, kaempferol, a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties, gallic acid, a polyphenol that has antimicrobial and antioxidant effects, and catechin, a flavan-3-ol that has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, all of which contribute to the plant's potential therapeutic applications, including wound healing and anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Rosa woodsii subsp. arizonica are tea, tincture, salve, infused oil, and infusion. Rosa woodsii subsp. arizonica tea, prepared by infusing the leaves and flowers in hot water, has been traditionally used to treat digestive issues and reduce inflammation. The tincture, made by steeping the plant material in a solvent such as ethanol, is used to treat various health conditions including fever and respiratory issues. An infused oil, prepared by steeping the flowers in a carrier oil such as olive oil, is used topically to soothe skin irritations and wounds. The infusion, made by steeping the plant material in cold water, is used as a calming agent to reduce stress and anxiety.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Rosa woodsii subsp. arizonica improperly include developing skin irritation due to the presence of allergenic compounds and essential oils, which can cause redness, itching, and inflammation. If an individual experiences an allergic reaction, it may manifest as hives, swelling, or difficulty breathing, which can be life-threatening in severe cases. Using the plant improperly may also exacerbate diarrhea in some individuals, particularly those with pre-existing gastrointestinal issues, due to the potential presence of irritant compounds. Furthermore, consuming or applying Rosa woodsii subsp. arizonica may trigger an allergic reaction in individuals who are sensitive to the plant's constituents, leading to symptoms such as rash, fever, or respiratory problems. Aggravating skin irritation is also possible if the plant's extracts are not used correctly, causing prolonged inflammation and discomfort.
